rationale: I created a mid-sized grid (14 cards) to balance breadth and discoverability: large, commonly used professional identities (Sociology, Political Science, Anthropology) are top-level, while applied and interdisciplinary practices (Public Policy, Development Studies, STS) get their own cards when practitioners commonly self-identify that way. I deliberately kept Education Research and Communication Studies separate from Sociology and Political Science because they follow distinct literatures, methods, and outlets. I avoided very narrow topic niches (e.g., Migration, Health Sociology) and instead placed them under broader, professionally named headings to keep each card large enough for a practical audience picker. Some splits are judgment calls (e.g., Public Policy vs Political Science; Area Studies vs Development) and were resolved by privileging professional identity and typical journal outlets.

subspecialties.name: Sociology
subspecialties.tagline: Social structure, organizations, inequality, and social theory
covers: General sociology and subfields (family, work, stratification)
Examples: urban sociology, social theory, inequality studies

subspecialties.name: Anthropology
subspecialties.tagline: Cultural, social, and archaeological studies of people
covers: Cultural anthropology, ethnography, archaeology, linguistic anthropology
Examples: ethnographic fieldwork, material culture, kinship studies
explicitly_excludes: Area Studies — regionally focused interdisciplinary programs

subspecialties.name: Political Science
subspecialties.tagline: Comparative and domestic politics, institutions, political behavior
covers: Comparative politics, political theory, public opinion, institutions
Examples: party systems, voting behavior, legislative studies
explicitly_excludes: Public Policy — policy design and evaluation work
International Relations — foreign policy and global security

subspecialties.name: International Relations
subspecialties.tagline: State behavior, security, diplomacy, and global governance
covers: Security studies, foreign policy, international organizations
Examples: war and peace studies, international law, diplomacy
explicitly_excludes: Development Studies — economic and social development research

subspecialties.name: Education Research
subspecialties.tagline: Teaching, learning, policy, and educational outcomes research
covers: K–12 and higher education studies, assessment, pedagogy
Examples: randomized trials of interventions, education policy analysis
explicitly-excludes: Sociology — sociological analyses of schooling (if general rather than pedagogical)

subspecialties.name: Communication Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Media, rhetoric, interpersonal and mass communication research
covers: Media studies, journalism research, political communication, rhetoric
Examples: media effects studies, campaign communication analyses
explicitly-excludes: Computer Science — computational media / NLP (goes elsewhere)

subspecialties.name: Criminology
subspecialties.tagline: Crime, criminal justice systems, policing, and corrections research
covers: Patterns of offending, justice policy, forensic sociology
Examples: policing studies, recidivism evaluations, crime measurement
explicitly-excludes: Law — doctrinal/legal scholarship (goes to Law/Legal Studies if present)
Psychology — forensic psychology (goes to Brain & Cognitive Sciences)

subspecialties.name: Human Geography
subspecialties.tagline: Spatial social science: place, environment, and urban processes
covers: Urban studies, spatial inequality, environmental and economic geography
Examples: land-use studies, spatial demography, GIS-based research

subspecialties.name: Linguistics
subspecialties.tagline: Language structure, use, acquisition, and variation research
covers: Phonetics, syntax, sociolinguistics, language acquisition
Examples: corpus studies, field linguistics, dialectology
explicitly-excludes: Brain & Cognitive Sciences — neurolinguistics and psycholinguistics

subspecialties.name: Science and Technology Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Socio-cultural study of science, tech, and innovation systems
covers: STS theory, histories of science, technology governance
Examples: laboratory studies, innovation policy, platform studies
explicitly-excludes: Public Policy — policy implementation when not STS-focused

subspecialties.name: Demography
subspecialties.tagline: Population dynamics, fertility, mortality, and migration studies
covers: Population statistics, projection methods, migration analysis
Examples: census-based studies, life-table analyses, migration flows
explicitly-excludes: Sociology — qualitative population studies without demographic methods

subspecialties.name: Development Studies
subspecialties.tagline: International development, poverty, and program evaluation research
covers: Economic and social development, NGOs, impact evaluations
Examples: cash transfer RCTs, development policy analysis, community interventions
explicitly-excludes: Economics — macro/structural economic modelling (goes to Economics & Finance)

subspecialties.name: Public Policy
subspecialties.tagline: Policy analysis, evaluation, governance, and implementation studies
covers: Policy design, program evaluation, regulatory studies
Examples: cost-benefit analyses, policy experiments, implementation research

subspecialties.name: Area Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Region-focused interdisciplinary social and cultural research
covers: Regional expertise and interdisciplinary work centered on a world area
Examples: Latin American studies, East Asian studies, African studies
explicitly-excludes: Development Studies — development-focused work in a region

uncovered_concerns: Migration studies: considered as its own card but folded into Demography or Sociology because standalone volume is often below threshold.
Health sociology / medical sociology: often tagged under Sociology or Public Policy rather than a separate card to avoid fragmentation.
Law / Legal Studies: important adjacent field, but usually separated from core Social Sciences here (would live in a Law/Legal category if present).

Digital/social data science (computational social science): frequently overlaps with Communication, Political Science, and STS; kept distributed rather than a separate card to preserve MECE professional identities.
Environmental social science: overlaps with Human Geography and Development; not split out to avoid thin cards.
